region_of_origin: Singapore (Indian diaspora cuisine; dish originates from North India / Punjab)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for butter chicken prata set. FOODON_00002764 (chicken curry dish) is the closest defensible parent. The prata/paratha flatbread component is not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken meat, cooked, with sauce) is the closest defensible parent for the butter chicken component. The prata flatbread component is not represented. Composite set meal not directly coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (chicken curry) is the closest available term. Butter chicken prata set as a composite meal with flatbread is not individually coded.

main_ingredients: prata (plain flatbread made from flour, ghee, egg, water)

serving.description: 1 set (butter chicken curry with 2 pieces of prata)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 250 g of butter chicken curry (sauce and chicken pieces) and two pieces of plain prata (approximately 100 g each), as typically served in a Singapore Indian restaurant set meal.

nutrient_source.title: Composite estimate: Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) per ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods + Plain Paratha per ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; weighted by serving component proportions
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Butter Chicken Prata Set' as a composite restaurant meal. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for butter chicken (murgh makhani) and plain paratha in a 250 g : 200 g ratio (curry : prata), then expressing the blend per 100 g. Butter chicken per 100 g reference: energy ~130 kcal, protein ~12 g, fat ~7 g, carbohydrate ~5 g, sodium ~420 mg. Plain paratha per 100 g reference: energy ~326 kcal, protein ~8 g, fat ~12 g, carbohydrate ~48 g, sodium ~290 mg. Weighted blend per 100 g of combined 450 g serving: energy ~210 kcal, protein ~10.4 g, fat ~9.1 g, carbohydrate ~22.4 g. Values are estimates; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Analogous estimate: Chapati/Paratha GI ~52 (University of Sydney GI Database) and butter chicken curry component GI ~45 (low-GI protein-fat-rich curry); weighted composite GI estimated at ~57 for the combined set meal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Butter Chicken Prata Set. Plain prata/paratha GI is estimated at ~52 based on chapati GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002; chapati GI=52, reference glucose). Butter chicken curry component is protein- and fat-rich with low carbohydrate content; GI estimated at ~45 by analogy with other Indian meat curries. Weighted composite GI for the set (200 g prata contributing ~96 g carbohydrate, 250 g curry contributing ~12.5 g carbohydrate) yields an estimated GI of approximately 57. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (57 × 100.8 g) / 100 ≈ 57.5. Carbohydrate per serving: prata 200 g × 48 g/100 g = 96 g; curry 250 g × 5 g/100 g = 12.5 g; total ≈ 108.5 g available carbohydrate; GL = 57 × 100.8 / 100 ≈ 57.5. This is an estimate; direct GI testing of this composite meal has not been published.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~370 mg per 100 g; ~1665 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard set or request reduced-salt preparation, as a full serving provides approximately 72% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~57.5) driven by the prata flatbread component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider limiting prata to one piece per meal and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the glycemic load, as the full set provides approximately 108 g of available carbohydrate with a medium estimated GI of 57.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter, cream, and ghee (~4.2 g per 100 g; ~18.9 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that a full serving provides approximately 18.9 g of saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 14–20 g, and should consider requesting a lighter cream preparation or reducing portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Chicken Prata Set is a popular Indian-Singaporean restaurant meal combining two distinct dishes: butter chicken (murgh makhani) curry and plain prata (paratha) flatbread. The butter chicken component consists of tender chicken pieces marinated in yoghurt and spices, pre-cooked in a tandoor or pan, then simmered in a rich, mildly spiced tomato-cream-butter sauce flavoured with garam masala, kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves), garlic, and ginger. The sauce is characteristically smooth, orange-red in colour, mildly sweet, and creamy. The prata is a layered, pan-fried flatbread made from wheat flour, ghee, egg, and water, with a crispy exterior and soft, flaky interior. In Singapore, this set is commonly served at Indian Muslim restaurants and mamak-style eateries, reflecting the South Asian diaspora culinary tradition. Nutritionally, the set is energy-dense (~210 kcal per 100 g; ~945 kcal per full serving), moderate in protein (~10.4 g per 100 g), and contains significant saturated fat from butter, cream, and ghee (~4.2 g per 100 g). The prata component contributes the majority of carbohydrate (~48 g per 100 g of prata), resulting in a high glycemic load per serving (~57.5). Sodium content is moderate to high (~370 mg per 100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Butter Chicken Prata Set as a composite restaurant meal. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN reference data for butter chicken (murgh makhani) and plain paratha in proportion to typical serving weights. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for chapati/paratha and protein-fat-rich curry components. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite estimate from approved primary sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods data for butter chicken and plain paratha, weighted by a 250 g curry : 200 g prata serving ratio. Direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant set meal has not been performed. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for these components. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for these components. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for these components. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this composite meal has not been published. 6. Halal status reflects typical Singapore Indian Muslim restaurant practice; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from typical fatty acid profiles of butter, cream, and wheat flour components; these are not directly reported in ICMR-NIN for this composite. 8. The cuisine_tags field from the input included 'Naan' which is not accurate for this dish; prata/paratha is the correct flatbread type and this has been corrected in the record.
